Before the age of mortals, the Moon was a flawless sphere of polished silver. Its guardian, Lunis, was a vain spirit who spent his nights admiring his own reflection in the calm oceans of the nascent Earth. His light was so piercingly bright that the lesser stars faded into obscurity, unable to compete with his blinding perfection.

Astraea, the ancient Weaver of Stars, grew weary of Lunis’s arrogance. Her celestial tapestries were being ignored, her delicate constellations washed out by his overwhelming glare. One evening, she gathered a handful of primordial meteors—rough, jagged stones forged in the furnace of creation. As Lunis preened over the Pacific, Astraea hurled the stones across the cosmos.

They struck the silver sphere with deafening, silent impacts, shattering the perfect surface. Deep craters and sprawling dust seas scarred Lunis’s once-immaculate face. Shocked, he tried to buff away the blemishes, but the marks were permanent. 

Humbled, his light softened from a blinding glare to a gentle, milky glow. Now, the stars could shine alongside him. To this day, the Moon wears his scars as a reminder that true beauty lies not in flawless vanity, but in sharing the infinite night sky.

The Sun ruled the day with a heavy, golden hand, but the Moon, young and timid, feared the vast emptiness of her nocturnal domain. Enter Zephyr, the spirit of the twilight wind. Pitying the restless earth, Zephyr flew to the edge of the horizon just as the Sun was setting. He caught the last, dying embers of the day in his silver pouch. Racing back to the night sky, Zephyr blew the embers across the diamond-studded canopy. 

The warm breath of the sun-fire didn't melt the stones; instead, it set them spinning. As they turned, they caught the light of the Moon, flashing and fading in an eternal cosmic dance. Thus, the stars learned to twinkle, bringing a gentle, rhythmic peace to the slumbering world.

Yet, as the sun retreated further and the icy bite of winter approached, these fragile creatures grew weary. The freezing gales threatened to extinguish the remnants of the celestial starlight and spring warmth they carried in their gossamer wings.

Seeking refuge from the encroaching frost, the butterflies fluttered into the dense, ancient forests. They huddled tightly against the rough, dark bark of the towering pine trees, shivering as the first snowflakes began to fall. The Great Pines, ancient and wise, felt a deep pity for the beautiful creatures. To protect the lingering magic, the trees wept thick, golden tears of warm resin. 

The glowing sap flowed over the resting butterflies, enveloping them in a protective, honey-colored embrace. As the long winter froze the earth, the resin hardened into brilliant, translucent stones. This was the creation of the first amber. Deep inside these golden gems, the brilliant colors of the butterflies remained perfectly suspended. To this day, when sunlight catches a piece of amber, it reveals the eternal, unbroken magic of ancient springs and starlight preserved within the earth.
